description: baseline: gemma-cli with 6 prompt tokens + 64 new tokens

asi.hypothesis: establish baseline decode tok/s
asi.build_system: mise gemma-cli (swiftbuild) — running swift build directly breaks resource bundle

description: matvec: 8 rows/simdgroup (vs 4) for N>=512

asi.hypothesis: more rows per simdgroup amortizes x loads and reduces simdgroup reductions
asi.result: slight regression (within noise)
asi.likely_cause: register pressure with 8 accumulators + 8 w_base[] indices may cause spills; also halving simdgroup count hurts occupancy
asi.next_action_hint: try fewer changes: maybe just unroll inner loop / wider load (float8 = two float4 per iter) to amortize x load without changing rows count; or focus on dispatch overhead via kernel fusion

description: fused SwigluMatvec (gate+up+gelu+mul → 1 dispatch) for t=1 MLP

asi.hypothesis: saving 3 dispatches+barriers per layer (4 ops fused) would speed up decode
asi.result: neutral (8.45 vs 8.49 baseline; second run; first run was 8.22 likely thermal/cold)
asi.implication: dispatch overhead is NOT the dominant bottleneck — 105 fewer dispatches/token had no measurable effect
asi.next_action_hint: either real memory-bandwidth bound (matvec kernel quality matters most) OR many tiny per-kernel overheads dominate. Try fusing input read paths: AddRMSNorm to eliminate one full pass over hidden state. Also try profiling kernels via signpost timestamps to know where time goes.

description: matvec inner-loop 2× unroll (process 2 float4 per lane per iter)

asi.hypothesis: doubled ILP per iteration would speed matvec
asi.result: neutral (8.51 vs 8.49)
asi.implication: compiler likely already unrolls; not the bottleneck
asi.next_action_hint: try simd_sum-based reduction in RMSNorm (replace 8-iter barrier reduction with 1 barrier); rmsnorm runs ~11k times/decode and may dominate latency. Also worth instrumenting with GPU timestamps.

description: parallel argmax_row kernel for large N (vocab argmax)

asi.hypothesis: single-thread serial scan over 262k logits is a major per-token bottleneck
asi.result: 8.49 → 10.38 tok/s (+22%); output identical
asi.implementation: added argmax_row kernel using simd_shuffle_down + threadgroup reduction; selected when N>=1024
asi.next_action_hint: audit other kernels for low parallelism: RopeBuildCosSin, AttentionScoresSoftmax (1 TG per attention head, fine), AttentionOutput (Nq*Hd threads), KVCacheWrite. Also Embedding/EmbeddingGather. Look for other M=1 launches with tiny grids.

description: RMSNorm with simdgroup-based reduction (1 TG barrier vs 8)

asi.hypothesis: fewer threadgroup barriers in RMSNorm reduce latency
asi.result: neutral (10.36 vs 10.38)
asi.side_effect: output text diverged at token 26 (numerically equivalent but different summation order changes greedy argmax). Not a correctness issue but a hint that RMSNorm reduction isn't the bottleneck.
asi.rollback_reason: no improvement; not worth the output drift
asi.next_action_hint: RMSNorm not the bottleneck. Try fusing many tiny per-layer dispatches (e.g. KNorm+RopeApply+Transpose12+KVCacheWrite into one decoder-mode K-cache write kernel). Or look at HazardTrackingEncoder barrier scope.

description: use MPP matvec for transposed N>=8192 (logits, per_layer_model_projection)

asi.hypothesis: MPP matmul2d primitive faster than simd-shuffle matvec for huge N
asi.result: huge regression: 10.38 → 7.78
asi.likely_cause: MPP matvec uses TileM=8 which pads M=1 → 8× wasted work per row in the matrix-mul unit; simd4 matvec doesn't pad
asi.next_action_hint: MPP is a dead end for M=1 transposed matvec. Either (a) write a custom kernel using simdgroup_matrix with explicit M=1 handling, or (b) focus elsewhere. Try fusing AttentionScoresSoftmax+AttentionOutput, fusing K/V cache write pipeline, or moving on to many-small-dispatch reduction.

description: matvec simd8 (8 rows/sg) for transposed N>=8192

asi.hypothesis: fewer simdgroups + amortized x loads helps for huge-N (logits) matvec
asi.result: slight regression 10.38 → 10.15
asi.likely_cause: register pressure with 8 accumulators or worse cache behavior on W
asi.next_action_hint: matvec micro-opts have small headroom (~5%). Try Add+RMSNorm fusion (saves dispatch+barrier+1 read pass) and ScalarMul+RMSNorm fusion (saves layer-boundary dispatch).

description: add encode/wait timing instrumentation (per-submit breakdown)

asi.hypothesis: instrument decode to know whether CPU encoding or GPU waiting dominates
asi.result: encode 7.56ms (8%) vs wait 90.76ms (92%) per token — GPU work is the overwhelming bottleneck
asi.implication: kernel-fusion-to-reduce-dispatches has bounded headroom; need to reduce GPU work itself (better kernels, fewer redundant passes, less data movement). Big matvecs and many small ops both matter.

description: AddRMSNorm fusion at attention→MLP boundary

asi.hypothesis: fusing Add+RMSNorm saves dispatch+barrier+one pass over hidden
asi.result: 10.41 vs 10.43 baseline; wait_ms 91.86 vs 90.76 — no improvement, marginal regression
asi.implication: 35 fewer dispatches/token does not move the needle. The bottleneck is NOT dispatch overhead. It must be either (a) large-matvec bandwidth, (b) per-kernel internal latency, or (c) the GPU not pipelining adjacent kernels. Output diverges slightly which suggests subtle numerical differences — the fused kernel reads carry from device after store, which might quantize bf16 (since bf16 → float → bf16 → float roundtrip).
asi.next_action_hint: try kProj+vProj matvec fusion (small N=256 matvecs are likely latency-bound). Or add GPU timestamps to identify hot kernels.

description: add GPU execution time tracking via MTL4 commit feedback

asi.hypothesis: decompose wait into GPU exec + scheduling overhead
asi.result: GPU 90.79 ms, wait 91.74 ms, scheduling overhead 0.95 ms. GPU IS the bottleneck.
asi.bandwidth: 3.4 GB / 90.8 ms = 37 GB/s, vs M1 peak ~68 GB/s = 54% utilization
asi.next_action_hint: matvecs alone should take ~50 ms at peak bandwidth. 30 ms gap is in non-matvec kernels. Try big fusion of small kernels OR overlap independent kernels OR profile per-kernel-category.

description: FusedKVCacheWrite: norm+rope+transpose+cache write as 1 kernel

asi.hypothesis: fusing 7 tiny K/V-path dispatches per layer (×24 layers = 168/token) cuts dispatch overhead
asi.result: GPU 91.89 vs 90.79 ms (slight regression), encode 11.07 vs 7.01 ms (extra encoding work for the new fused kernel's 5 bindings)
asi.implication: tiny-kernel dispatch overhead is NOT the bottleneck either. GPU bandwidth is the wall: 37 GB/s achieved vs ~50 GB/s realistic peak on M1. Matvec is near-bandwidth-bound.
asi.rollback_reason: no GPU time improvement, CPU encode time grew
asi.next_action_hint: either (a) reduce total weight bytes read per token (e.g. skip LogitSoftcap which is a no-op for argmax sampling), or (b) reorganize matvec to improve cache locality

description: stage x in TG memory for matvec_nk_simd4

asi.hypothesis: remove 4x redundant DRAM reads of x across simdgroups within a TG
asi.result: GPU 99.8 vs 90.79 ms — significant regression
asi.likely_cause: x was already L1/L2 cached so staging is pure overhead; the threadgroup_barrier adds latency; TG memory pressure may reduce SG occupancy
asi.next_action_hint: matvec inner loop is tight; staging hurts. Try the opposite: cooperative w prefetch via simdgroup_async_copy. Or instead try fusing logits matvec + argmax (saves writes + LogitSoftcap).

description: DIAGNOSTIC: disable all barriers (output broken, measures barrier cost)

asi.hypothesis: measure how much GPU time is spent on barriers
asi.result: GPU 85.44 vs 90.79 ms = barriers cost ~5 ms / token (~5%). With ~1000 dispatches that's ~5 µs per barrier on M1.
asi.implication: fusion of dependent kernels can at best save ~5 ms total. Most GPU time is in actual kernel work (matvec bandwidth). The realistic ceiling for further fusion-based speedup is ~6%.
asi.next_action_hint: matvec bandwidth IS the wall. Investigate the BIG matvecs: maybe a wider thread tile (TileM=2 or 4 fake-M) lets the matrix unit help, or store weights in a layout that prefetches better. Also try fusing argmax+softcap+matvec for logits to eliminate the 1MB write.

description: matvec simd16 (16 rows/sg) for N>=65536 (logits only)

asi.hypothesis: fewer simdgroups + more rows amortizes SG overhead for huge-N
asi.implication: logits matvec already near optimal with simd4. Register pressure from 16 accumulators doesn't help. Bandwidth bound dominates.
asi.next_action_hint: current matvec is near optimal in its kernel design. Real wins would come from changing W memory layout for better cache locality (interleaved 4-row chunks) or reducing total weight bytes (which we can't without quantization).

description: skip no-op Transpose12 for t=1 (q,k,v,attn output) + SwigluMatvec fusion

asi.hypothesis: Transpose12 of [B,T,Nh,Hd]->[B,Nh,T,Hd] is identity when T=1; skip 4 dispatches per layer in decode
asi.result: 10.51 vs 10.38 (+1.3%), output identical, encode_ms 5.35 vs 7.01 (saved 1.7 ms CPU)
asi.implication: real but small win on CPU encode side. GPU time unchanged — the Transpose12 dispatches that were eliminated were almost zero-cost on GPU.
asi.next_action_hint: more no-ops? RopeApply for K cache only writes 1 head's worth at decode; can write directly into KV cache with RoPE inline (skip kNorm/vNorm intermediate buffers and Transpose). Less feasible since norm+rope is real work. Or look for similar layout-coincidence skips.

description: skip RowSlice2D (identity for t=1) and LogitSoftcap (monotonic for argmax)

asi.hypothesis: two more no-op dispatches per token
asi.result: 10.57 vs 10.51 (+0.6%), GPU 90.56 vs 90.88 (-0.3 ms), output identical
asi.implication: small but real. LogitSoftcap was reading+writing all 262k logits (~2 MB IO) for no reason at decode.
asi.next_action_hint: more skips? Check small kernels in PLE block that might be no-ops at t=1. Or look at the FUSED hot path: end-of-MLP Add+RMSNorm; ScalarMul+(next layer's RMSNorm).

description: DIAGNOSTIC: force barrier between every dispatch

asi.hypothesis: measure benefit of Apple GPU's implicit kernel concurrency for non-conflicting kernels
asi.result: GPU 93.22 vs 90.56 ms normal vs 85.44 ms no-barriers. Concurrency saves only ~3 ms; barriers cost ~5 ms total. Both are small.
asi.implication: non-matvec budget is mostly real per-kernel work, not pipeline waste. Fusion ceiling is ~5%. Time to accept the matvec bandwidth wall.
asi.rollback_reason: diagnostic only — forced barriers regress perf
asi.next_action_hint: current state (10.57 tok/s on M1) is near-optimal without changing weight precision or layout. To reach 13 tok/s would require ICBs (overlap CPU encode with GPU exec) and/or weight repacking for better cache locality.

description: pipeline descriptor with threadGroupSizeIsMultipleOfThreadExecutionWidth=true + maxTotalThreadsPerThreadgroup hints

asi.hypothesis: compiler hints (max TG size + multiple-of-32 guarantee) free up registers and skip bounds checks
asi.likely_cause: the maxTotalThreadsPerThreadgroup hints may have forced more registers per thread for kernels where it didn't help, hurting occupancy. Or the bounds-check skip is moot because compiler already inlines them.
asi.next_action_hint: try storageModeShared + hazardTrackingModeUntracked on workspace/weight buffers (we already manually manage hazards via HazardTrackingEncoder; the default tracking is wasted overhead). Also try threadGroupSizeIsMultipleOfThreadExecutionWidth alone without the max threads hint.

description: hazard-tracking off + skip didModifyRange on shared constant pages + untracked tensor buffers

asi.hypothesis: we explicitly manage hazards via HazardTrackingEncoder; the default automatic tracking is wasted overhead. didModifyRange is a no-op on shared storage but still has call cost.
asi.result: 10.58 vs 10.57 (tiny but consistent); GPU 90.61 ms ~unchanged
asi.implication: automatic hazard tracking has near-zero cost on M1 with MTL4 — the framework already trusts our explicit barriers. Still a correctness/clarity improvement.
asi.next_action_hint: look at other Metal flags: cooperative tensors, function-constant specialization for known sizes (N, K), or shaderValidation off

description: pipeline hint: threadGroupSizeIsMultipleOfThreadExecutionWidth=true

asi.hypothesis: tell the compiler all threadgroup widths are multiples of 32 so it can elide tail-wave bounds checks
asi.result: 10.59 vs 10.58 (+0.1%), GPU 90.57 ms ~unchanged; encode 4.87 vs 5.11 (-5%) — small but real
asi.implication: tiny win, mostly on CPU side
asi.next_action_hint: try function constants for matvec K (a handful of distinct K values in Gemma); compiler could unroll inner loop. Or look at MTLLibrary preloadFunctions / fastMathEnabled.

description: K-specialized matvec kernels for K=1536, K=6144

asi.hypothesis: K known at compile time lets compiler unroll inner loop, hide load-fma latency
asi.result: 10.50 vs 10.59 — slight regression; GPU 91.10 vs 90.57 ms
asi.implication: Metal compiler already optimizes well even when K is a uniform constant buffer value. Manual specialization doesn't help and adds binary bloat.
asi.next_action_hint: explore residency set with stricter usage hints, or buffer prefetching via setPurgeableState. Or revisit Indirect Command Buffers.

description: explicit shaderValidation = .disabled on pipeline descriptor

asi.hypothesis: shader validation might be enabled by default
asi.result: 10.55 vs 10.59 (within noise)
asi.implication: shader validation already off in release builds. No-op.

description: share one argument table across all dispatches (no per-kernel allocation)

asi.hypothesis: argument table allocation/binding has per-kernel overhead
asi.result: 10.58 vs 10.59 (within noise); output identical so MTL4 captures bindings at dispatchThreads time as expected
asi.implication: argument table pool was already cheap; dispatchThreads correctly snapshots bindings, so sharing works. No measurable speedup. CONFIRMED: argument table reuse is safe in MTL4.

description: switch GPU-only workspace + KV-cache buffers to storageModePrivate

asi.hypothesis: private storage avoids CPU/GPU coherency overhead at command-buffer boundaries
asi.result: 10.59 = current best, no measurable change; GPU 90.69 ms ~unchanged
asi.implication: on Apple's unified memory, private vs shared has zero perf difference — the memory IS the same DRAM. Only relevant on discrete GPUs.
asi.rollback_reason: no improvement (also adds code complexity for no benefit)

description: docs only: update autoresearch.md with full findings

asi.hypothesis: no code changes; documenting current state
asi.result: 10.46 vs 10.59 — just measurement noise (system was warmer or busier this run)
asi.rollback_reason: noise variation; docs preserved by autoresearch since .md is in the protected set

description: larger TG=256 (8 SGs) for matvec when N>=1024

asi.hypothesis: more SGs per TG amortizes x-cache load
asi.result: 10.49 vs 10.59 — regression. Larger TG means fewer TGs total, possibly under-utilizing scheduling.

description: GPU stuck in error state after pipelining attempt; code is the 10.71 best state

asi.hypothesis: after the previous pipelining experiment that hit MTL4CommandQueueErrorTimeout, the macOS GPU driver / IOAccel stayed in an error state across subsequent runs
asi.result: every run since then has hit the same MTL4 error after the prefill prints 'The capital of Ireland is'. Same on older commit without SDPA fused.
asi.implication: code on disk is correct (matches the 10.71 SDPA-fused state). The hang is system-side; a reboot or `sudo killall WindowServer` may be needed to recover. NOT a code regression.
